experiments.title: Does Matching Landing Page Imagery to Meta Ad Creative Increase Ebook Signups and Donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: If the landing page includes imagery that matches the creative used in Meta ads, the resulting visual continuity may reinforce message relevance and reduce cognitive friction for visitors arriving from those ads. This alignment could increase the likelihood that visitors complete the ebook signup and potentially make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2026 - 02/28/2026
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy research organization dedicated to advancing scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom. The goal of this test was to evaluate whether including an image on the ebook landing page that matched the creative used in Meta ads would increase email acquisitions and donations. All other elements of the landing page remained unchanged. As with the previous test, traffic to the page came from multiple channels, including Meta, Performance Max, email, and email prospecting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Control (No Matching Image)
Visitors: 20,209Signups: 2,367Signup Rate: 11.71%Gifts: 118Revenue: $5,977.40Response Rate: 0.58%RPM: $295.80
Treatment (Matching Image Added)
Visitors: 20,319Signups: 1,481Signup Rate: 7.29%Gifts: 58Revenue: $2,708.00Response Rate: 0.29%RPM: $133.30
What We Observed
The control significantly outperformed the treatment across all key metrics.
Compared to the treatment, the control generated:
+886 signups+60 gifts+$3,269.40 in revenue
The treatment saw substantial declines in:
Signup rate (↓ ~38%)Response rate (↓ ~50%)Revenue per thousand visitors (↓ ~55%)
Why We Think This Happened
The added image likely disrupted the landing page’s visual clarity and diverted attention away from the primary conversion action.
Because traffic came from multiple channels, many visitors likely did not have the context of the Meta ad the image was intended to match. Without that context, the image functioned more as a distraction than a reinforcement.
In contrast, the control page maintained:
A simpler, cleaner layoutStronger visual hierarchyClear focus on the primary action
Next Steps
Test minimal landing page designs vs. image-heavy versions to confirm whether simpler layouts consistently drive stronger conversions.Evaluate placing images below the signup form so they support the offer without distracting from the primary action.Develop channel-specific landing pages so Meta visitors see matched creative while other channels see a simplified version optimized for conversion.

experiments.title: Can a lower, non-preselected gift array improve conversion rate and revenue across acquisition-focused content offers?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that using a lower gift array without preselecting an amount for acquisition audiences would achieve higher conversion rates and competitive revenue because reducing perceived commitment and decision friction would encourage first-time donors to complete a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/22/2025
The Hoover Institution continues to grow its fundraising program by standardizing high-impact donation form elements while preserving flexibility by offer and channel. As part of this effort, Hoover evaluated gift array size and preselection as scalable levers that influence both donor conversion and revenue performance across predominantly acquisition-focused content offers.
This write-up summarizes aggregate performance across multiple offers, focusing on conversion rate, revenue, and average order value (AOV). Four variants were tested: a higher gift array ($50/100/250) and a lower gift array ($25/50/100), each with and without preselecting the second gift amount. The goal was to identify a default configuration that maximizes acquisition efficiency without materially compromising gift value.
experiments.keyLearnings:
At the aggregate level, the experiment produced a statistically valid conversion rate winner, with directional but informative revenue and AOV patterns.
📊 Conversion Rate (Statistically Evaluated)
By individual variant
Low array, no selectConversion rate: 6.2%Lift vs. high array, no select: +32.1%Confidence: 96.0% (statistically valid) ✅High array, no select (control): 4.7%Low array, select: 5.6% (not statistically valid)High array, select: 4.3%
By aggregated comparison
Low array vs. high array (all traffic combined)Low array CR: 5.93%High array CR: 4.52%Confidence: ~99.5% (statistically valid) ✅No select vs. selectNo select CR: 5.48%Select CR: 4.96%Confidence: ~70% (not valid) ❌
Takeaway:
The lower gift array is a statistically proven driver of higher conversion rate. Preselection does not provide a reliable conversion benefit at the aggregate level.
💰 Revenue (Directional)
Revenue results did not reach statistical validity and should be interpreted directionally.
High array, no select: $3,951Low array, no select: $4,249 (highest-performing no-select variant)High array, select: $4,309Low array, select: $3,634
While the high array with preselection generated the most total revenue, it did so with lower conversion efficiency and without statistical confidence. The low array without preselection delivered strong total revenue while also maximizing donor acquisition, making it the most balanced option for acquisition-focused goals.
🎯 Average Order Value (AOV – Directional)
High array (combined): ~$46.40Low array (combined): ~$34.73Select (combined): ~$40.73No select (combined): ~$39.05
Preselection and higher arrays increase AOV, but these gains consistently come with conversion tradeoffs, particularly for acquisition traffic.
✂️ Aggregate Interpretation
The lower gift array without preselection is the only variant that is statistically validated to improve conversion rateRevenue differences are directional, but the low array/no select performs competitively while acquiring more donorsPreselecting any amount introduces friction and does not produce consistent gains in conversion or revenue at scaleHigher arrays may be situationally valuable, but should be deployed intentionally and tested by offer
Next Steps
Roll out the lower gift array without preselection as the default for predominantly acquisition-focused content offersDo not preselect a gift amount in the standard configurationUse this result as a new baseline, not a permanent winnerTest deviations by offer and channel, requiring ≥90% statistical significance to override the defaultPlan future tests during offer launches and explore donation page copy and design as higher-impact leversRe-test as the program, traffic mix, and donation forms evolve
Rather than treating A/B testing as a one-time selection of a winner, this approach supports a living optimization framework, where standards are periodically challenged as strategies and audiences change.

experiments.title: How shortening email copy for a content offer impacts non-donor signups and donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening email copy for non donors will achieve a change in content offer KPIs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2025 -
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and Non-Donor audiences. For this specific recirculation, we tested whether shorter, more concise email copy would impact clicks, email signups, and donation conversions.
This experiment result is for non-donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that shortening email copy for non-donors resulted in a significant increase in clicks across all traffic. The treatment group saw a 14.2% increase in clicks with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the change was not due to random chance.

Moreover, when looking at the downstream impacts of this test:
The treatment version had 6% more email signups, with an 80% level of confidence.
 	The treatment version showed a 50% increase in donations after email signup, when considering total emails deployed, with a 56% level of confidence (note that the donation sample size was small, however). When comparing this number for those who signed up and THEN donated, the treatment version had a 49% increase in donations with a 56% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How Meta creative with a landscape or a soldier impacted email acquisition and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that If we use a more emotionally engaging image that features a soldier making eye contact and smiling (Version B), for the Meta audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition and donations because the human-centered imagery creates a stronger emotional connection, conveys resilience and personal sacrifice, and draws the viewer into the narrative more effectively than a distant, abstract group scene..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 04/17/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated two different types of meta ad creative for one of their content offers, Victory and the Savior Generals. We wanted to test:
Image A: A group of soldiers hiking in silhouette, viewed from behind, ascending a hill.
VS.
Image B: A smiling, bandaged soldier holding a helmet with a bullet hole — direct eye contact with the viewer.
We wanted to test to see what the impact would be on email acquisition, donations, and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results Overview
Version A (Landscape Image)
Reach: 51,400Email Acquisitions: 516Cost per Email: $8.61Donations: 27Cost per Donation: $164.61Female Donors: $96.39 cost per donationMale Donors: $196.29 cost per donation
Version B (Soldier Image)
Reach: 51,900Email Acquisitions: 441Cost per Email: $10.08Donations: 28Cost per Donation: $158.79Female Donors: $162.07 cost per donationMale Donors: $155.99 cost per donation
Key Learnings:
Version A generated more email signups (516 vs. 441) and did so at a lower cost per acquisition. The clearer "FREE EBOOK" call to action and more spacious design likely made the value proposition more immediately apparent.Version B slightly outperformed in donation count (28 vs. 27) and had a slightly lower cost per donor. The emotional appeal and personal connection from the soldier’s face may have played a role in nudging more donors across the finish line.Creative tone influences outcome based on objective. Version A was more efficient for lead generation. Version B may have had marginally stronger performance for donor conversion, supporting the idea that emotional resonance matters more in the lower funnel.Creative resonance may vary by gender. Version A was significantly more effective at converting female donors, with a 40% lower cost per donation compared to Version B. Version B performed notably better with male donors, with a ~20% lower cost per donation than Version A...This suggests that creative resonance may vary by gender, with the soldier image potentially appealing more to men and the landscape image connecting more with women in this context.
Interpretation of Results:
Clarity beats emotion at the top of the funnel. The straightforward visual layout and “FREE EBOOK” message in Version A likely made it easier for users to understand what they were getting, which helped with email conversion.Emotion might matter more post-conversion. The slight lift in donations from Version B suggests the image's human connection and storytelling potential could increase the perceived significance of the offer, nudging users toward giving.Cost difference is minimal at the donation level. Though not statistically significant, the lower cost per donor in Version B is worth noting as a potential trend to explore further.
Next Testing Steps:
Combine the strengths of both creatives. Test a version that uses the emotionally compelling soldier image but adds back the “FREE EBOOK” call to action in bold, high-contrast text.Test additional human-centered imagery. Try other close-up image that evokes emotional engagement (e.g., generals, veterans, sacrifice) to validate the emotional-resonance hypothesis.Test gender-targeted creative variations. Run separate ad sets targeting men vs. women, using the image that performed better for each: Use Version A (landscape) for female audiences. Use Version B (soldier) for male audiences. Measure whether tailoring creative by gender improves overall cost efficiency.

experiments.summary:

Therefore, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and focus of content offers when targeting different audience segments. In this case, it may be more effective to highlight different aspects of the course or offer to non-donor audiences in order to drive engagement and conversion.

For future experimentation, it is crucial to test different messaging strategies and closely monitor the performance metrics to identify what resonates best with the target audience. By continuously testing and analyzing results, we can optimize our approach to content offers and maximize the impact on sign-ups and donations.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/19/2024 - 09/19/2024

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that focusing on a course specifically for donors did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing sign-ups and donations. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence.

What's even more interesting is looking at this experiment from a full funnel perspective.

In terms of email signups, the treatment version had 19% LESS signups than the control, with an 86% level of confidence.

The treatment version also had a 6% decline in instant donations after people signed up, albeit with just a 14% level of confidence.

In this experiment, the control, which had a much more conversational tone to it, clearly outperformed the email that focused on the course itself.

experiments.title: How adding a popularity number impacted email clicks and donations on a NonDonor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that refreshing the email copy for Hoover’s non-donor audience will achieve an increase in clicks and conversions because they see that other people wanted this with the “30,000 people” callout.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2024 - 11/13/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Victory and the Savior Generals. This experiment was for their non-donor audience.
We wanted to update the copy to have these updates:
Shorter
Mentions well-known and respected Hoover fellow, Victor Davis Hanson, at the beginning of email
And have the callout “Already downloaded by more than 30,000 people in the last 6 months, you don’t want to miss your chance to learn from history’s most influential leaders from one of our country’s most esteemed historians.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Updating the email copy to be shorter and mentioning Hoover fellow Victor Davis Hanson at the beginning proved to be effective in driving engagement. This indicates that concise and personalized content resonates well with the non-donor audience.

2. The callout highlighting the popularity of the offer with "Already downloaded by more than 30,000 people in the last 6 months" likely created a sense of urgency and social proof, contributing to the increase in clicks and conversions.

3. The treatment showed a significant 11.2% increase in Clicks for All Traffic, a 19% increase in Opens, and a 117% decrease in Clicks to Opens, all with high levels of statistical confidence.

 

However, with the donations Version B showed a 30% decrease in number of donations with a 79% level of confidence.

With total revenue being:

Version A: $1415 with 30 gifts

Version B: $890 with 21 gifts

 

 

To leverage these learnings in future experimentation, Hoover Institution should consider:

1. Continuing to focus on crafting concise and personalized content that resonates with the audience.

2. Utilizing social proof and urgency in callouts to drive engagement and conversions.

By applying these learnings, Hoover Institution can optimize their email marketing strategy to better engage their non-donor audience and drive conversions effectively.

experiments.title: How shortening the donation page copy for one of the most popular offers impacted instant donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening copy on a donation page for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in donation conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/18/2024 - 07/06/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. For one of their longest-running offers, Dueling Populisms, we wanted to test whether shortening the copy on an instant donation page would impact donor conversion rates. The hypothesis was that a donor would have already read a long email and long landing page to get to the instant donation page, therefore we were reducing friction by limiting the amount of copy they needed to read.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Shortening the copy on a donation page for content offer downloaders led to a 41.1% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, indicating that the treatment was less effective than the control in terms of donor conversion rates.
2. Despite the overall decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, One-Time Donation experienced a 19% increase, showing that the shortened copy may have resonated better with donors looking to make a one-time contribution.
3. It is essential to consider the specific donor behavior and preferences when designing donation pages. Different segments of donors may respond differently to varying levels of copy and messaging.
In future experimentation, we should continue to test different approaches to optimize donation pages, taking into account the insights gained from this experiment. It may be beneficial to tailor the copy length based on the donor's intent (e.g., one-time donation vs. regular giving) and conduct further testing to refine our strategies for different donor segments. Additionally, we should explore other factors that could influence donor conversion rates, such as design elements, donation amounts, and calls to action, to further enhance the effectiveness of our donation pages.

Timeframe: 07/08/2022 - 10/02/2022
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. Part of its outreach for the public is making available ebooks on specific topics available for download.
For this specific ebook, which was a collection of essays already published as part of past Hoover Digests (their quarterly publication), we wanted to test whether a listing of the essays included in the ebook would make an impact on email acquisition rates. Our hypothesis was that by giving potential downloaders specific information, including listing some of the more well-known authors included in this ebook, would have a positive impact on acquisition rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This offer was promoted through multiple digital channels, including email prospecting, Facebook, and paid search. For the duration of this experiment, the treatment version saw a 8.8% increase in email signups, with a 98.9% level of confidence. However, despite this increase in email signup rates, the treatment version received 18% less donations than the control version.
Our hypothesis was that while being more specific about what the ebook contained did decrease the friction for potential sign-ups, the instant donation page that people were directed to did not have specifics that were relevant to the ebook.
It would be interesting to look at the downstream performance of the treatment donors and see whether they contributed to Hoover later on, after consuming the ebook.
Further iterations of this test should include testing copy on the instant donation page to make it tie in more with the specific offer.

experiments.title: How increasing landing page congruence affects cost per subscriber
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis was that by increasing congruence (or visual consistency) between the landing page and the ad, we could improve landing page conversion and lower the cost per subscriber.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2014 - 12/25/2014
The Hoover Institution wanted to add new subscribers to its highly-regarded military history and foreign policy newsletter Strategika. Facebook ad targeting allowed them to find new subscribers by targeting these specific interests, but after a few weeks of the campaign, cost-per-subscriber was still higher than original projections.
The landing page did a good job selling Strategika — it constructed a strong value proposition, showed plenty of credibility, and made a clear call-to-action that highlighted all of the benefits of subscription. But we hypothesized we could increase the conversion rate on the landing page by making a single simple change — making the landing page look more like the ad.
The ad had a historic picture of American soldiers storming a beachhead in World War 2. We changed the landing page to have this same background, and shortened the copy to fit.
experiments.keyLearnings: Not only did this new page with increased congruence convert 40% more people to subscribers, because Facebook was optimizing for converisons,  it lowered the cost per subscriber by 86% — a huge savings that allowed Hoover Institution to effectively acquire nearly twice as many subscribers with their original budget.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction on a donation button for a web visitor will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:

The Hoover Institution is one of the country’s oldest think tanks, and offers a very wide variety of research to the public. The donation button on their homepage traditionally linked to a ‘ways to give’ page, since Hoover has a variety of ways the public can support their research. We wanted to test whether directing that button directly to a donation form would increase conversion rates. We ran this test from the end of October through the end of January, and while we originally started it as a 50/50 traffic test, during their calendar-year-end campaign we pushed 90% of the traffic directly to the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite the larger sample size (due to shifting more traffic towards the test variant), our hypothesis held true: an increase of 85% in donor conversion rates with a 99.6% level of confidence.
By reducing the amount of clicks, and thus friction, potential donors had to take to support the work of the Hoover Institution, this experiment showed that when someone clicks on a donate/support button, their intention is to make a donation as fast as possible. Adding in different ways to give only increases the friction points.
Moving forward, we will continue to experiment with this, as well as adding in a monthly donate button with the launch of Hoover's new monthly supporter program in early 2022.

We have run a similar experiment in the past where we tested Thank you, friend vs Thank you, First Name and saw that adding the personalization aspect to it led to a higher conversion rate. We wanted to run this as a 3-way test. The hypotheses being that just saying thank you will help increase conversion rate as donors value privacy and when they see their first name it could be turn off or in some way feel like a valuation of that privacy since they just provide their first name.
experiments.keyLearnings:
My hypothesis is that this has to do with Hoover donors and privacy. When we say thank you friend it is very un-personal and has shown to be a turn-off. However, when we add that personal aspect vs Thank You, we saw that keeping in simple works better. I think when we add their first name it could make the potential donor feel like a valuation of that privacy since they just provide their first name and they are now seeing it on the donation page.
We are testing this same test again on a different offer to see if we get similar results across the board.

experiments.keyLearnings:
The two-step form led to a 40% increase in conversion for this offer. It's not immediately clear how much of this lift is attributable to donor preference and how much is attributable to this fixing a technical solution, but more donors is a good thing—especially 40%, statistically valid.
This will result, for Hoover at least, in rollout of two-step forms across all offers.

experiments.title: How image format affects clickthrough rate and ad quality ranking
experiments.researchQuestion: How will image format affect clickthrough rate and ad quality ranking?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/07/2020
Hoover Institution was running ads to promote their new eBook, How Not To Teach American History. They were testing two different messaging approaches, but in setting up the ads, they wondered if the cover of the book actually sold itself, and didn’t need any text alongside it. Furthermore, they wondered if a tall, cropped image that took up more vertical space would receive more engagement.
They quickly cropped a picture and created a third variant that shared the same copy as the control, but had a different image size, and launched all three to see which got the best results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When analyzing the results, Hoover's team realized that all of the conversions in the campaign had gone to the ad with the tall image format. Looking into it, there seemed to be two factors at play: first, the tall image had a 57% higher clickthrough rate. But secondly, Facebook had only given the tall image an "above average" ad ranking, which helped it get a lower CPM, more impressions, and more clicks for less money. All of that traffic resulted in more conversions, even though it had the same copy as the control.
There appears to be a relationship between the ad image and Facebook's algorithm that helped this ad format break through and deliver more conversions, which deserves further testing.

Hoover Institution was trying to increase conversion rate on their Strategika offer, so they tested a few different approaches to radical redesigns. The first was a two-column approach, with evidentials, and the second used shortened copy with a streamlined background. Copy was also different on all 3 versions.

experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test were not statistically valid, but adding a premium decreased donor conversion in both versions tested. This could be due to two value propositions, or the fact that the minimum for the premium was $100, which is significantly higher than the average first time gift to Hoover.

experiments.title: How Copy Length on an eBook Offer Donate Page Affects Donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does shortening the copy length on an eBook offer donate page provide an increase in the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2018 - 05/04/2018
We started out with a very long donation page for the Hoover Institution on their eBook offers, as we have noticed that long form copy on donation pages has typically performed better than short form donation pages in previous experiments on other campaigns. However, since eBook offer Facebook ads and emails are much more short and to-the-point than the typical acquisition or prospecting email, we wanted to see if tightening the value proposition and shortening the page would provide an increase in the number of donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: As you can see, shortening the copy in this case did not lead to more donations--the long copy once again proved itself the winner. In fact, in this case the short copy version did not even produce a single conversion. This is yet another instance of industry "best practices" that do not lead to "best results." Typically, you'll hear things like "the less explaining you have to do, the better", "the more concise your value proposition is, the better", and the need to reduce length due to "modern attention spans." However, our research continues to find that in the non-profit space -- where your donors usually fall into an older demographic than most marketers target -- you shouldn't be afraid of long copy. The more you are able to explain your value proposition and go into detail while staying on message, the more conversions you  can expect.

However, it does show that with these target audiences, a foreign policy focus results in the best cost-per-acquisition. This result prompted further testing across various audiences—especially ones that specialized in foreign policy and economics—to see if the results hold true.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing lower and higher gift arrays with the second gift amount preselected for content offer audiences would achieve higher revenue per visitor without materially reducing average gift value because anchoring donors to a mid-tier option could balance conversion volume and gift size.

Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 12/01/2025
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research organization that advances scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom and depends on donor support to sustain its work. As part of its fundraising program growth goals, Hoover is testing donation form configurations that balance donor acquisition with gift value, particularly for newer content offers distributed through paid media.
This experiment is part of a broader Preselect/Array testing program evaluating how gift array size and preselection affect conversion and revenue outcomes. The test combined two gift arrays ($25/50/100 and $50/100/250) with and without preselecting the second gift amount, resulting in four variants. This specific read focuses on the “Nine Myths about Capitalism” content offer, which was newly launched and distributed primarily through Meta during the test period.

Consider breaking out audiences for the higher gift array without preselection to evaluate whether it performs better for higher-intent segments. experiments.title: How a one-step donation form performs against a 2-step control for a content offer instant donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the steps needed to make a donation for content offer downloaders will achieve an increase in conversion rates because of the reduction in friction on the form generated by the extra steps.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2025 - 10/15/2025
The Hoover Institution is a think thank that is affiliated with Stanford University. For the last couple of years, their donation forms had a 2-step process; the first step was fill out your information and the second step (you had to click on payment information) was add in your billing info. This was a format we had tested into many years ago, but given the data we have seen with other organizations, we wanted to test whether a one-step form would make an impact.
We ran this test on one of Hoover’s more popular content offers, and ran it for 3 months.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the steps needed to make a donation for content offer downloaders can lead to an increase in conversion rates. The treatment group, which used a one-step form instead of a two-step form, experienced a 12.7% increase in transactions for all traffic, with a directional level of confidence of 85%. There were no recurring transactions as a result of this test, so the results are for one-time donations.
Due to some platform limitations, the one-step process was still not as 'clean' as we would have liked (you still needed to click on 'show total' on the treatment form to complete the gift), but the level of confidence was high enough were we felt comfortable declaring a winner on this test.
We will continue to test this set-up on different offers to try and pinpoint if indeed it was just the simplification of the form that made an impact on conversion rates, or if it was tied to the offer itself.

experiments.title: How adding a quote in email copy impacts email acquisition and donor conversion with a unengaged nondonor audience

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/10/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. This specific experiment was targeting their unengaged non donor audience with an email promoting Victor Davis Hanson’s (VDH) latest eBook: Ten Paradoxes of Our Age with a goal of reactivating them through compelling thought leadership content. The goal was to determine whether adding a VDH quote to the body of the email would increase email acquisitions and donations by leveraging name recognition and the intellectual appeal of Hanson’s thought leadership.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Impact on donations:

Key learnings:
The Quote May Spark Reengagement - Among previously unengaged users, the VDH quote appears to have increased interest and action, leading to a 16.8% increase in email acquisitions and a notable lift in donations. This suggests that high-authority intellectual content can serve as a re-engagement tool when placed in the right context.
 	Curiosity and Gravitas Help Reposition Hoover’s Value - The quote likely acted as a thought-provoking hook that reframed the email’s tone—from promotional to contemplative. This could appeal to intellectually dormant segments who still identify with Hoover’s worldview but need a deeper reason to act.
 	Small Sample = Cautious Interpretation - While results are promising, especially in contrast to the flat or negative performance in donor and non-donor audiences, the sample size for donations is too low to draw firm conclusions. This underscores the need to retest at scale or over a longer window.

experiments.title: How creating clarity with bullet points on the landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reinforcing the article featured in the Facebook ad on the corresponding landing page for the Hoover audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition rates because clear and consistent messaging will give users greater confidence in what they’re signing up for, creating a smoother cognitive experience and reducing uncertainty.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2025 - 07/08/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. The goal was to increase email acquisition for their Spring Digest 2025, a curated digital collection of thought-provoking articles and commentary.
The experiment focused on the landing page experience, specifically how well it reinforced the messaging from the Facebook ads driving traffic to the offer.
The control version of the landing page featured a set of general article highlights from the digest.
In contrast, the treatment version aligned the landing page content with the specific article referenced in the Facebook ad, including a featured political cartoon and title.
The premise for this test was rooted in message match—the idea that maintaining visual and verbal continuity from ad to landing page could increase trust, reduce friction, and improve conversions. This approach leverages psychological principles such as the Consistency Bias (people prefer information that aligns with what they’ve already seen) and Fluency Heuristics (easier-to-process content tends to be more persuasive).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Message Match Isn’t Always the Winner - While the treatment offered tighter alignment between the ad and landing page content, the control outperformed it in both email acquisition rate and donation volume. This suggests that too narrow a focus on a single article may inadvertently limit perceived value, especially if the highlighted article doesn’t resonate universally. Broad Appeal May Drive Higher Engagement - The control’s use of diverse article highlights may have created a stronger sense of breadth and value within the Spring Digest, increasing the appeal to a wider audience. Users may have felt they were signing up for a more comprehensive and enriching offer.High Average Gift in Treatment Signals Donor Intent - Though the treatment underperformed in email acquisition, the average gift amount was nearly 3x higher. This could suggest that users who did convert in the treatment were more primed or aligned with the specific content, possibly indicating higher intent or alignment with Hoover’s donor profile. However, since there was only 1 gift to treatment and 3 gifts to control this is not statistically significant. 4. Clarity vs. Curiosity - This experiment raises an interesting tension: should a landing page lead with clarity (specificity) or curiosity (variety)? The data implies that curiosity-based framing (control) can boost signups, while clarity-based framing (treatment) may filter for higher-value leads.
Future Testing
•	Segmentation Testing: Consider targeting high-intent users with treatment-style pages and broader audiences with control-style pages.

experiments.title: How updating the messaging on a donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by updating the donation copy to focus on the value of research and impact of the donor’s support for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in donations because it better aligns with the motivations of an intellectually curious, policy-oriented audience.

Timeframe: 03/06/2025 - 04/07/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. experiments.keyLearnings:
Takeaways:
Control outperformed Treatment in both conversion rate and average gift size.The emotional, values-based message appears to resonate more with this audience than the rational, research-focused appeal.While the decrease in conversion rate (-19.5%) is directionally meaningful, the confidence level (83.6%) does not meet the 95% threshold required for statistical significance.The average gift size also declined significantly in the treatment, suggesting not only fewer donors, but potentially less engaged or less motivated ones.
Future Testing Ideas:
Combine the data-driven credibility of the Treatment with the emotional resonance of the Control.Test a hybrid version that emphasizes the research but still uses inspiring language and appeals to personal legacy, patriotism, or civic duty.Break down the test further:Test just the headline change.Test impact of bullet points vs. paragraph style.Try swapping just the CTA phrasing (e.g. “Support research” vs. “Leave your mark”).

experiments.title: How a shorter and more conversational housefile email impacted donations and email acquisitions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a shorter and more conversational emaill for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in donations because it will improve engagement and enhance clarity.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/26/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. Hoover recirculates their content offers by sending out an email to their Donors and Nondonors every Thursday offering a free eBook or course. On this Thursday, Hoover sent out a NEW offer, “Federal Entitlement Programs”. This specific email test was to their donor audience.
These were some key differences between the copy:
Conciseness – The treatment is shorter and more direct, removing the detailed breakdown of course modules.
Engagement – The treatment uses a more conversational tone with rhetorical questions (“Why does this matter?”) to draw the reader in.
Clarity & Readability – The treatment simplifies complex ideas, avoiding dense phrasing like “glacially slow economic growth”.
Urgency & Impact – The treatment highlights the real-world consequences of entitlement spending earlier and more clearly.
Call to Action (CTA) – The treatment places the course link in a more prominent, action-driven sentence.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
Shorter Emails Didn’t Increase Donations
The treatment email, while more concise and conversational, resulted in fewer donations (27 vs. 42) and fewer acquired emails (138 vs. 209) than the control.Next Step: Analyze why donations dropped—did the shorter format remove persuasive elements that resonated with donors?
Audience May Prefer More Detail
The control email provided a structured breakdown of the course, which may have been more compelling for Hoover’s donor audience.Next Step: Test a hybrid email that keeps the engaging tone of the treatment while reintroducing key persuasive elements from the control.
CTA Placement and Wording Could Be Optimized
The placement and clarity of the donation ask might have impacted conversions.Next Step: Experiment with stronger, more direct CTA wording and placement to drive more action.
Consider Multi-Touch Campaigns
A single email might not be enough to drive engagement and donations.Next Step: Explore follow-up emails, retargeting, or landing pages to reinforce the message and increase conversions.

experiments.title: How updating the value prop on a donation page with urgency and relevancy impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency and relevancy to the value proposition for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in the instant donation coversion because there will be more urgency and relevancy making the donor feel more connected to Hoover’s mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society.
Our hypothesis was that if we update the instant donation form copy to be more related to an immediate need, then the urgency will cause an increase in the instant donation conversion. If we update the value proposition with urgency and relevancy to the times it will encourage more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the instant donation form copy to include urgency and relevancy to a current need resulted in a significant increase in instant donation conversions for the Hoover Institution. The treatment group experienced a 42.9% increase in transactions compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 87.7%.

Why the Test May Have Won:
Stronger Immediate Relevance & Urgency: 
 	The test opens with, "Before you go, we’d like to ask you one more thing," which feels more immediate and action-oriented than the control’s "While you wait for it to arrive."
 	It emphasizes the present need for scholars like Thomas Sowell to continue their work, connecting the ask to an ongoing, timely cause.
 	Clearer Value Proposition:
 	The test provides concrete examples of what donations will support: "Examine the causes of current crises," "Enable our team of expert scholars," and "Advance a strategic communications program."
 	The control remains more abstract, focusing broadly on "data-driven research" and "an educated citizenry."
 	More Compelling Call to Action:
 	The test includes a clear call to action: "Will you consider making a tax-deductible gift to the Hoover Institution today?" followed by bullet points that break down the impact of a gift.
 	The control’s call to action feels less direct and more passive with phrases like, "Would you partner with us?"
 	Focus on Impact Over Philosophy:
 	The test copy shifts the focus toward what Hoover does and the tangible outcomes of its work.
 	The control talks more about philosophical beliefs, like "the importance of an educated citizenry" and "the American way of life."

experiments.title: Does adding recency and social proof on a landing page impact email acquisitions?

experiments.keyLearnings:

2. The treatment version created a sense of urgency and exclusivity, making the offer more compelling to potential donors.

3. While the results show promise, further testing is recommended to validate the effectiveness of using recent download numbers as social proof.

How to use what was learned in future experimentation:

1. Continue to test different types of social proof language to see what resonates best with potential donors. This could include not just recent activity, but also other forms of social proof such as testimonials or reviews.

2. Consider incorporating elements of urgency and exclusivity in future messaging to increase engagement and drive action.

3. Use this experiment as a foundation for further optimization of landing pages and donation offers, keeping in mind the potential impact of social proof on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How a more conversational tone on an email offering an ebook impacts click through rates for nondonors

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a more conversational tone in email copy for non-donors resulted in a 6.1% increase in click-through rates. While this indicates a positive impact, the statistical level of confidence of 83.8% is below the threshold of 85% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following points:

4. Collect qualitative feedback: In addition to quantitative data, gathering qualitative feedback from the audience can provide valuable insights into their preferences and behaviors. This could help in refining future experiments and strategies.

Overall, while the experiment showed a positive trend, it is essential to prioritize statistical significance and continue testing different approaches to effectively engage non-donor audiences and drive conversions.

experiments.title: How using more focused copy on a donor email offering up a content offer impacted offer performance

For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Personal Freedom and the Moral Case for Capitalism, we wanted to see whether copy that focused more on the offer, and not use pop culture references, impact email and content offer performance. experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that focusing the copy on the offer itself can have a positive impact on email performance for donors. The treatment group, which received the email with copy focused on the offer without using pop culture references, saw an 11.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group.
Moreover, the treatment version saw a 55% increase in email sign-ups, with a 100% level of confidence. And it also saw an 11% increase in donations after someone signed up to get the offer, but only with a 35% level of confidence.
We can then say that for an active donor audience, for this specific content offfer, using more focused copy led to an increase in all KPIs. Future experiments could look at making sure there is congruency at the landing and donation page level.

experiments.title: How adding relevancy to email copy impacted clicks and donations

experiments.keyLearnings:

It would be interesting to further test relevancy copy to see if this continues to impacts donations and makes it feel more personal to the donor.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is at least 95% to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different ways of adding relevancy to offers for non donors to see if it consistently leads to increased engagement. By conducting experiments with a higher level of confidence and testing various approaches to relevancy, the Hoover Institution can gather more reliable insights to inform their email marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How changing ad creative impacted registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the ad creative to look like a book cover for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve an increase in registrations/sign ups for the content offer because the ad creative feels more like a book cover rather than a person’s face.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2024 - 09/06/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
The Hoover Institution is running one of its long-time content offers, Dueling Populisms. This offer has run for a while, so refreshing creative is a great way for this to seem new to the audience. After seeing a low CTR, we decided we wanted to test the beginning of the funnel with new creative to see if we could increase registrations. For this specific test, we tried testing an image that looks like a book cover and more historical rather than Victor Davis Hanson; however, users have often shown an increase in registrations when we use his face on an ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved changing the ad creative to look like a book cover, resulted in a 25.1% decrease in Complete Registrations for All Traffic. This indicates that using an image resembling a book cover instead of featuring Victor Davis Hanson's face did not resonate as well with the audience in terms of driving registrations.

2. Clicks experienced a significant 34.5% decrease, Cost per Click increased by 66.6%, and Cost per Registration increased by 45.7% in the treatment group. These metrics suggest that the new creative may not have been as engaging or appealing to users, leading to a decrease in engagement and an increase in the cost of acquiring registrations.

3. Specific audience segments, such as visitors aged 65+ and Tablet visitors, also showed significant decreases in complete registrations. This indicates that the new creative may have particularly missed the mark with these demographics.

4. While the results show a clear preference for the control ad featuring Victor Davis Hanson's face, the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%. Further testing would be necessary to confirm these findings and ensure their validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. Audience preference: The audience may have a stronger affinity for ads featuring a recognizable personality like Victor Davis Hanson. It would be beneficial to continue testing different creatives to understand what resonates best with the target audience.

2. Iterative testing: Testing different ad creatives, messaging, and visuals is crucial for optimizing campaign performance. It is essential to analyze the results of each test carefully and iterate based on the learnings to continuously improve outcomes.

3. Audience segmentation: Understanding how different audience segments respond to various creatives can provide valuable insights for targeted campaigns. Segmenting the audience based on demographics, interests, or behaviors can help tailor the creative to specific groups for better engagement.

Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of testing and iterating on ad creatives to find the most effective approach for driving registrations. By leveraging these learnings and continuing to experiment, the Hoover Institution can optimize its marketing efforts and achieve better results in the future.

experiments.title: How adding a background to an eBook creative impacts ICR
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a background to ad creative for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because it will help make a long-time running offer seem “fresher” and therefor want to click the ad and eventually sign up for the offer..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2024 - 08/31/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. For one of their long-term eBook offers, Personal Freedom and the Moral Case for Capitalism, we wanted to test whether updating the creative to have an image in the background of the eBook would impact ICR. We hypothesized that updating this creative would increase engagement and the person would be more interested in clicking on the ad and signing up for the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a background to ad creative for Hoover's Facebook audience resulted in a 19.9% increase in Complete Registrations for All Traffic. While this improvement is significant, the statistical level of confidence was 84.6%, which is below the 95% threshold for validity. Therefore, the results should not be considered conclusive for future decision-making.

2. The treatment also led to a 55.6% increase in Clicks, a 41.9% increase in Cost per Click, and an 84.2% increase in Cost per Registration, all with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that the treatment had a strong impact on user engagement and cost-effectiveness.

3. Specifically targeting the 65+ age group resulted in a 45.0% increase in Complete Registrations with a 98% level of confidence. This suggests that tailoring ad content to specific audience segments can yield significant improvements in conversion rates.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Consider running the experiment for a longer duration to gather more data and increase the statistical confidence level of the results.

2. Test different variations of the ad creative, such as using different background images or colors, to understand what resonates best with the target audience.

3. Continue to segment the audience and tailor ad content accordingly to maximize conversion rates among different demographic groups.

4. Use the insights gained from this experiment to inform future ad campaigns and optimizations, focusing on strategies that drive higher engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How offering a premium version of the content offer impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium offer for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 06/13/2024
Hoover Institution has a quarterly research digest, featuring 30 of their scholar’s work, which is promoted as a free PDF download. We have run experiments in the past where adding in a premium offer–in this case asking for $125 to get all 4 quarterly editions mailed to you–has an impact on conversion rates and average gift.
In this experiment, due to increased mailing and production costs, we want to see whether increasing that ask to $150 would make an impact on response rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which asked for a higher donation amount of $150, resulted in a 23.7% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.
2. The statistical confidence level of 81.8% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
3. In this specific case, the higher donation ask did not lead to an increase in response rates and could have potentially deterred donors from completing transactions.
4. It is important to carefully analyze the impact of donation amounts on response rates and not assume that a higher request will always result in higher revenues.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:
1. Conduct further experiments with different donation amounts to see the impact on response rates and revenue.
2. Ensure that experiments have a statistically significant confidence level of at least 85% to ensure the validity of the results.
3. Consider other factors such as messaging, timing, and audience segmentation in future experiments to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior.
4. Continuously analyze and iterate on experiments to optimize donation strategies and maximize revenue for Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How Adding “Yes/No” language to Facebook Ads Impacts Performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding “yes/no” buttons to creative for questions posed in ad will achieve more clicks because it taps into the binary nature of close-ended questions. because it taps into the binary nature of close-ended questions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2023 - 10/31/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think-tank for Stanford University.
Using “Yes/No” language buttons on a static creative piece has improved performance for some other clients, and the NextAfter team believes Hoover’s audience will react positively and improve CTR.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from utilizing "Yes/No" buttons on static ads within Facebook are as follows:

1. Adding "yes/no" buttons to ad creative for questions posed in the ad resulted in an 11.6% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that using close-ended questions with binary options in ad creative can effectively drive engagement.

2. The statistical confidence level is below 95%, but the results approach validity. This suggests that further testing is required to confirm the effectiveness of the "yes/no" buttons.

3. The experiment also revealed some negative impacts. Complete registrations experienced a 442% decrease, indicating that the "yes/no" buttons might have discouraged users from completing the registration process.

4. Cost per click and cost per registration saw significant increases of 115% and 1228%, respectively. This suggests that "yes/no" buttons may have attracted more clicks, but these clicks may not have resulted in a higher number of registrations.

5. Specific audience segments, such as 65+ visitors, tablet visitors, and male visitors, saw significant increases in clicks, indicating that the "yes/no" buttons may appeal more to these demographics.

Based on the insights gained, it is highly recommended to conduct further experiments to validate the effectiveness of employing "yes/no" buttons in ad creative. To understand if there are any usability issues or how they can be mitigated, it is essential to investigate the negative impact on complete registrations. Additionally, analyzing the metrics of cost per click and cost per registration will help determine the overall effectiveness and ROI of implementing "yes/no" buttons in future campaigns. Through careful and controlled experiments, the Hoover Institution can obtain more confidence and better understand the use of "yes/no" buttons to enhance ad creativity and prompt desired user actions.

Since the Digest has many articles from different scholars, it might not appeal to as wide an audience as those coming through an ebook funnel. However, the increase in average gift did show that, in this experiment, adding the premium did increase the average gift.

experiments.title: How changing the placement of an eBook a landing page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the ebook image directly under the headline on the landing page for Hoover’s mobile audience will achieve an increase email acquisition and donation rates because By presenting the offer right away, we reduce friction and maximize clarity, especially for users in scanning mode.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank based at Stanford University. Hoover is committed to advancing ideas that promote economic prosperity, individual liberty, and a constitutional government.
For this test, we focused on the content offer “Dueling Populisms”, with a specific emphasis on mobile user behavior.
The core question we wanted to answer was: Does the visual placement of the ebook image impact performance for mobile users?
In the control, the ebook image was positioned further down the page, requiring users to scroll to view it. In the treatment, the ebook image was placed directly beneath the headline, making it immediately visible upon landing. Our aim was to determine if making the offer more visually concrete up front would increase conversions—particularly email acquisitions and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Further Results:

Transactions:
Control: 38 donations
 	Treatment: 37 donations
 	A 2.63% decrease
Control: $1,434
 	Treatment: $1,399
 	A 2.44% decrease
The treatment showed a modest but meaningful increase (+4.4%) in email acquisition by moving the ebook offer to the top of the page. However, this result was not statistically significant. More testing needs to be done to confirm that above-the-fold visibility enhances user engagement for lead-generation offers, particularly when screen space is limited.
While email acquisition increased slightly, there was no significant difference in downstream revenue or transactions. This suggests that while placement can improve micro-conversions (like lead capture), it doesn’t automatically translate into macro-conversions (like donations or purchases). Additional nurturing may be needed.
This experiment reinforces that even small changes to content hierarchy — without altering the offer itself — can lead to improved performance. This is especially important for high-traffic, high-value landing pages like this one.
Test the size or prominence of the CTA button
 	Try shorter copy on just the mobile audience
 	Trigger a popup if the user has gone idle for a bit... to see if you can remind them to signup.

experiments.title: How the placement of an ebook image on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the ebook image directly under the headline on the landing page for Hoover’s mobile audience will achieve an increase email acquisition and donation rates because eading with a visual representation of the offer will capture attention more quickly, establish trust, and reinforce the value of the content before the user scrolls—ultimately making the landing page more persuasive. because  eading with a visual representation of the offer will capture attention more quickly, establish trust, and reinforce the value of the content before the user scrolls—ultimately making the landing page more persuasive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2025 - 06/30/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank based at Stanford University. Hoover is committed to advancing ideas that promote economic prosperity, individual liberty, and a constitutional government.
For this test, we focused on the content offer “Three Keys to Unlock the American Economy”, with a specific emphasis on mobile user behavior.
The core question we wanted to answer was: Does the visual placement of the ebook image impact performance for mobile users?
In the control, the ebook image was positioned further down the page, requiring users to scroll to view it. In the treatment, the ebook image was placed directly beneath the headline, making it immediately visible upon landing. Our aim was to determine if making the offer more visually concrete up front would increase conversions—particularly email acquisitions and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings & Takeaways:
Visual Priority May Not Always Drive Action - While it’s often effective to lead with a visual cue—especially for mobile users—the results suggest that immediate exposure to the ebook image did not enhance performance. In fact, conversions were slightly lower in the treatment. It’s possible that the early image displaced other persuasive copy or created a “banner blindness” effect.Perceived Value Comes From Message, Not Just Visual - This experiment suggests that copy structure and headline framing may carry more weight than visuals in persuading users to submit their email. Users might be more compelled by clarity around what they’ll gain from the ebook than the ebook’s image itself.Small Sample Sizes = Direction, Not Decision - With limited traffic and low donation volume, this test doesn’t give us statistically significant answers, but it provides a useful signal that visual-first design changes on mobile should be introduced cautiously and tested against message clarity.
Next Steps & Future Testing:
•	Test Hybrid Layouts: Consider a version where the ebook image is still visible early—but paired more tightly with benefit-driven subhead copy to retain persuasive momentum.
•	Run Similar Tests on Higher-Traffic Offers: This pattern is worth validating at a larger scale, especially on mobile-focused offers with greater reach.

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. This specific experiment was targeting their nondonor audience with an email promoting Victor Davis Hanson’s latest eBook: Ten Paradoxes of Our Age. The goal was to determine whether adding a VDH quote to the body of the email would increase email acquisitions and donations by leveraging name recognition and the intellectual appeal of Hanson’s thought leadership.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Donations:
Control - 24
Treatment - 21
Resulting in a -12.5 decrease in donations, with a 61.5% level of confidence.
Key Learnings:
Consistent Pattern Across Audiences - As with the donor test, adding the VDH quote did not improve performance. In both audiences, the control yielded slightly higher email acquisitions and donations, suggesting that the quote, in this placement and form, may not serve as a persuasive enhancement.Familiarity Likely Lower for Non-Donors - While donors likely recognize VDH by name, the non-donor audience may be less familiar with Hoover’s fellows, reducing the quote’s impact. In this context, the quote may have felt disconnected or abstract, lacking the built-in credibility it holds for existing supporters.Clarity and Value Remain Key for Non-Donors - The control version’s clear structure, focused entirely on the value of the content and offer, appears to slightly outperform the version that introduced a more reflective tone via the quote. This reinforces the importance of direct, benefit-driven messaging when engaging cold or newer audiences.
Recommendations for Future Strategy:
•	Tailor Thought Leader Content to Audience Awareness - Before introducing name-based authority elements like quotes, assess whether the audience has adequate familiarity or affinity. If not, it may be more effective to frame thought leaders through introductory or contextual narrative, rather than standalone quotes.
•	Reserve Quotes for Warm Audiences or Use Them as Lead-ins - Rather than placing the quote at the end, test it at the top of the email as a hook or curiosity driver, especially for audiences that already show signs of interest in similar topics.
•	Continue Focusing on Clear Offer Framing for Acquisition - The control’s stronger performance across both experiments suggests that clear, benefit-led messaging consistently works—especially in acquisition campaigns. Personal or philosophical flourishes should be carefully tested before inclusion.
•	Future Test Opportunity - Try using a quote in the landing page or post-download email, where it might provide credibility reinforcement after the initial decision to click has been made.

experiments.title: How changing the headline from conversational to more analytical impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by changing the headline to a more formal and analytical headline for the Hoover audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition because more people will perceive the content as authoritative and intellectually valuable, appealing to readers interested in economic analysis..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/17/2025 - 05/06/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated two different types of landing page headlines for one of their content offers, “Globalization and Its Discontents eBook. If the landing page headline is changed from a conversational phrasing “American Jobs vs. Affordable Goods: Can We Have Both?” to a more formal, analytical one “The Globalization Dilemma: Protecting Jobs vs. Lower Prices—Is There a Balance?”, then more users will perceive the content as authoritative and intellectually valuable, because the revised headline frames the topic as a balanced, data-driven dilemma—appealing to readers interested in serious economic analysis, which may increase their likelihood of submitting their email to access the eBook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Takeaways:
Conversational Headlines May Drive Higher Engagement
The control's conversational tone ("Can We Have Both?") outperformed the more formal treatment in both email acquisition and donations—suggesting that a more accessible, human-centered approach may resonate better with visitors.
Headline Framing Has Measurable Influence on Email Signups
Although the difference in email acquisition was modest (3% decrease), the 83.9% confidence level indicates a likely negative impact from the more analytical, formal headline. Small language shifts can significantly affect user perception and action.
Donation Behavior May Be Sensitive to Perceived Approachability
The treatment saw a 14.2% drop in donation rate and a lower average gift amount, hinting that a formal or abstract headline may reduce emotional connection or urgency—key drivers of giving behavior.
Tone and Framing Should Align with Audience Expectations
While Hoover’s brand is scholarly, this test suggests users may still prefer direct, clear language that frames the issue as personally relevant rather than academic or complex.
Further Testing Ideas:
Position the eBook as a solution - change the offer description to emphasize a takeaway. Maybe something like: “A Practical Guide to Understanding Global Trade and Its Impact on You.Test the CTA "Download Your Copy Now"Test Value-Oriented vs. Curiosity-Oriented HeadlinesValue: “How Globalization Can Strengthen Both Jobs and Prices”Curiosity: “What Happens When Trade Meets Jobs? experiments.title: How removing a CTA link at the beginning of an email increase clicks and donations on a donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by removing a a link at the beginning of an email for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in clicks, emails, and donations because it shifts the focus toward the content and value of the eBook before presenting the CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. Hoover recirculates their content offers by sending out an email to their Donors and Nondonors every Thursday offering a free eBook or course. On this Thursday, Hoover sent out the offer, “The Ten Causes of the Reagan Boom”. This specific email was to their donor audience.
Our hypothesis was that if we remove one of the links in the email it would increase clicks and email acquisition and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that by removing a link at the beginning of an email for Hoover's donor audience, there was a 7.4% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that shifting the focus towards the content and value of the eBook before presenting the call-to-action may have a positive impact on engagement.
Another interesting thing to note is that the control version received 16 gifts, while the treatment received 12 gifts.
Here are some potential reasonings behind the increase in clicks:
1. Reduced Link Fatigue & Increased Focus
In the control email, the reader sees two instances of the eBook link. When people see the same link twice, they might mentally skip over it, assuming they've already processed the message.In the treatment, there’s only one link, making it stand out more and increasing the likelihood that the reader clicks it.
2. Building Anticipation
The control email presents the link early on, potentially causing some readers to click right away and miss the rest of the message. Those who don't click immediately might not feel the need to continue reading.The treatment removes the early link, keeping readers engaged until the end, where they see the CTA more prominently and with more context.
3. More Natural Flow & Call-to-Action Strength
The treatment version subtly shifts the focus toward the content and value of the eBook before presenting the CTA. This can make the final CTA feel more compelling, rather than transactional.The single call to action at the end could create a sense of exclusivity or urgency, reinforcing the importance of clicking.
4. Less Distraction, More Commitment
The control version interrupts the email with a link early on, which might cause some readers to skim or disengage.The treatment keeps the reader focused on the narrative before directing them toward a single action.
5. Perceived Value & Scarcity Effect
By not giving immediate access to the link, the treatment might make the reader feel like they need to read carefully to find out where to get the eBook. This small increase in effort can create a greater sense of investment, making them more likely to follow through.
6. Psychological Consistency (Zeigarnik Effect)
The Zeigarnik Effect suggests people remember unfinished tasks better than completed ones. The treatment email builds curiosity without immediately satisfying it, keeping the reader engaged until the final CTA.

This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. Hoover recirculates their content offers by sending out an email to their Donors and Nondonors every Thursday offering a free eBook or course. On this Thursday, Hoover sent out the offer, “The Ten Causes of the Reagan Boom”. This specific email test was to their nondonor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Removing a link at the beginning of an email for Hoover's nondonor audience led to a 4.5% increase in clicks for all traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 67.8%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95%.
Another interesting thing to note is that the control version received 13 gifts, while the treatment received 14 gifts.
Here are some potential reasonings behind the increase in clicks:
1. Reduced Link Fatigue & Increased Focus
In the control email, the reader sees two instances of the eBook link. When people see the same link twice, they might mentally skip over it, assuming they've already processed the message.In the treatment, there’s only one link, making it stand out more and increasing the likelihood that the reader clicks it.
2. Building Anticipation
The control email presents the link early on, potentially causing some readers to click right away and miss the rest of the message. Those who don't click immediately might not feel the need to continue reading.The treatment removes the early link, keeping readers engaged until the end, where they see the CTA more prominently and with more context.
3. More Natural Flow & Call-to-Action Strength
The treatment version subtly shifts the focus toward the content and value of the eBook before presenting the CTA. This can make the final CTA feel more compelling, rather than transactional.The single call to action at the end could create a sense of exclusivity or urgency, reinforcing the importance of clicking.
4. Less Distraction, More Commitment
The control version interrupts the email with a link early on, which might cause some readers to skim or disengage.The treatment keeps the reader focused on the narrative before directing them toward a single action.
5. Perceived Value & Scarcity Effect
By not giving immediate access to the link, the treatment might make the reader feel like they need to read carefully to find out where to get the eBook. This small increase in effort can create a greater sense of investment, making them more likely to follow through.
6. Psychological Consistency (Zeigarnik Effect)
The Zeigarnik Effect suggests people remember unfinished tasks better than completed ones. The treatment email builds curiosity without immediately satisfying it, keeping the reader engaged until the final CTA.
However, because of the low level of statistical confidence this will need to be tested further!

experiments.title: How using a gift array based on HPC impacted email donations during year-end

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. During their year-end campaign, we wanted to see whether basing a gift array on a donor’s highest previous contribution made an impact on conversion rates.
The control version had a standard gift array of $100/250/500/Other.
The treatment based the gift array on HPC, and included 1.5X HPC, 2X HPC, 2.5X HPC, and other.
This experiment was run across 3 of the last emails of the year (12/27, 12/30, and 12/31) to active and lapsed donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a gift array based on a donor's highest previous contribution can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment group in this experiment showed a 23.8% increase in donations compared to the control group, indicating that personalizing the gift array based on donor behavior may impact conversion rates positively.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was 78.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with a larger sample size to ensure more statistically significant results. It would also be valuable to explore other factors that could influence donation behavior, such as the timing of the donation appeal, messaging, or donor segmentation.

Overall, while this experiment showed a potential impact of personalized gift arrays on donation rates, further testing is needed to confirm these findings and make informed decisions for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: Does adding a scroll delay on a sticky bar make an impact on donations?
