experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/16/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
In the past, we’ve successfully tested using a sticky bar during a year-end campaign to help increase donor conversions on content offer donation pages. This year, we wanted to test whether the timing of when that bar shows up makes an impact. In this test, the control was displayed right when someone clicked onto that page; the treatment version had a 3 second delay and only showed up as people were reading the copy on the form.
Our hypothesis was that by timing the sticky bar, it would have more of an impact on donations if people had already started reading the copy on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The treatment, which involved delaying when a sticky bar renders for potential donors, resulted in an 8.1% increase in Transactions for All Traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 57.3%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.
3. Due to the lower statistical confidence, the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
4. Future experiments should focus on ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions or implementing changes based on the results.
5. It is essential to continue testing different variations to determine the most effective strategies for increasing donor conversions, considering factors such as timing and messaging on donation pages.

It is also essential to consider other factors that may influence donor behavior and tailor experiments accordingly.

experiments.title: How using a year-end specific sticky bar on acquisition pages impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar with year-end messaging for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in conversion rates because the urgency behind year-end giving deadlines.. because  the urgency behind year-end giving deadlines..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2023 - 01/01/2024
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think-tank for Stanford University.
During the crucial year-end fundraising season, we wanted to see whether we could impact conversion rates on content offer instant donation pages by using a year-end specific sticky bar.
We ran this on all content offer instant donation pages during the last 10 days of the year, as that is when Hoover sees the most revenue come in online in December.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with year-end messaging for content offer downloaders did not achieve the desired change in conversion rates. The treatment group actually saw a decrease in transactions by 12.2% compared to the control group.
It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for these results is only 79.8%, which is below the generally accepted minimum threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
While transaction conversion rates were lower for the treatment group, when looking at the impact on revenue, we see that the treatment version had a 104% higher average gift. Given the time of year this experiment was run, this is most likely due to a larger gift skewing results, but it is still interesting that the sticky bar with year-end messaging motivated that donor to make that large gift.

experiments.title: How Facebook creative impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by changing the creative to the authors photo for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher conversion rate because the authors are well known to the Hoover audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2023 - 08/25/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University.
Hoover runs many offers through Facebook and therefore we wanted to test a new approach to creative for ebooks. Hoover has many well known personalities that write the offers shared so we decided to test the standard 3d image of the offer vs an image with the authors photo.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment (using the authors' photo as the creative) resulted in a decrease in purchases for all traffic, indicating that it did not perform as well as the control (using a standard 3d image).

2. The level of confidence for the decrease in purchases is only 3.2%, which is below the acceptable threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

3. However, there were significant increases in other metrics such as clicks and complete registrations. Clicks increased by 184% with a 99% level of confidence, and complete registrations increased by 225% with a 95% level of confidence.

4. Cost per click and cost per registration also saw significant increases, with a 424% increase and a 376% increase respectively, both with a level of confidence of 100%.

Based on these findings, future experimentation could focus on leveraging the authors' photos in the creative for specific goals such as increasing clicks and complete registrations. However, it should be noted that the decrease in purchases suggests that using authors' photos may not be effective in driving actual conversions. Therefore, further experiments should be conducted to explore the impact on conversion rates and validity of results. Additionally, it could be valuable to assess the overall impact on campaign performance and return on investment when considering changes to the creative strategy.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition language on a content offer landing page impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because they know what they will be helping achieve by donating..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2023 - 04/23/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. As part of our efforts to drive new name and new donor acquisition through content offers, we wanted to run a test on landing page copy to see whether being more concise with what a donation would get would impact conversion rates. This test ran across all acquisition offers on all channels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity to potential donors may not necessarily result in an increase in conversions. The treatment resulted in a 6.2% decrease in donations with a 40.4% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better. However, since the statistical level of confidence falls below 85%, we cannot consider these results valid.
For future experiments, we can continue to test which specific points of clarity will help increase donor conversions. Since Hoover is a think tank, the immediate connection between a donation and a tangible impact is a harder case to make; continued language testing will help clarify that.

experiments.title: How a lock icon on a donation page impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the lock icon for prospective donors will achieve higher donor conversion because the perceived security has been increased.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2023 - 03/13/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University.
In an effort to increase donor conversion on donation pages, we decided to test the presence of a lock icon.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a lock icon to the donation page can lead to an increase in donor conversion. However, the statistical level of confidence is only at 43.2%, which is below the threshold of 85%, indicating that the results are not valid for future decision making. Therefore, it is necessary to run further experiments with an adequate sample size to validate these findings. Additionally, future experiments should consider testing other elements on the donation page that can lead to an increase in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How adding a premium offer to an ebook instant donation page impacts revenue.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for donors will achieve a change in average gift..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2022 - 11/11/2022
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. Part of its outreach for the public is making available ebooks on specific topics available for download.
Every quarter, Hoover publishes a collection of essays and articles in the Hoover Digest. There is a digital version of this that Hoover offers as an email acquisition tool, and there is a physical version of the Digest that gets mailed to donors who make a gift over $100. For this experiment, we wanted to test whether including getting access to the physical version all four quarters of the Digest, for a gift of $100, would increase the average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment brought in a slightly higher average gift than the control version, with an increase of 17% and a 77% level of confidence. This result is in line with previous premium-focused experiments we've run on the Hoover Digest.
Our hypothesis is that the positioning of the physical versions of the Digest is not as appealing to digital donors as more traditional offline donors. Further testing might including positioning the Digest premium test as providing a 'gift' for friends and family.

experiments.title: How adding a donors first name to the instant donation page can increase conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the first name for those that visit the page will achieve an increase in both donations and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2021 - 05/01/2021
In this experiment, we added a donor’s first name to the instant donation page once they downloaded the eBook it said “Thank you, First name” while the control just said, “Thank you, friend”. This experiment was run specifically on Facebook and we had a 50/50 split on who saw the control vs the treatment.
The main metric we were looking to track on this experiment was the donor conversion rate. Previously with Hoover we ran this experiment and saw a lift but we didn’t launch it across the board so we wanted to test it again as they had also recently changed their donation platform.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We had to stop this experiment early so it did not receive the necessary traffic it needed to validate (67%LoC) but with it seeing a 12% increase in conversion rate and a 43% increase in revenue (99% LoC) we will continue to test this across multiple Hoover offers.
People enjoy seeing their name and it adds that personal aspect and helps reinforce that they completed the signup.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 43.8% increase with a 99% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Organic Search visitors had an 8.3% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 14.3% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had a 322.3% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How simpliying donation page copy flow affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which version of the donation page copy and format resonates with more donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2018 - 01/10/2019
Hoover institution was running their Dueling Populisms offer with their classic control instant donation page, with bulleted gift impact statements. They wanted to test a version that removed the bullet points and rearranged some of the original copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the experiment did not validate, we did see a decrease in donation conversion from the treatment. This could be because the bulleted version lays out the reasons for giving in a more clear and concise manner.

experiments.title: How body copy length on a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Are more people moved to give by the promise of future offers/resources, or by the overall mission of the organization?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2018 - 07/30/2018
Hoover has been running the Dueling Populisms content offer for some time, and wanted to test 2 things: a shortened mission-focused value proposition, and a more offer-centric rather than mission-centric value proposition on their donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was not statistically valid, but both versions of the shorter copy increased donor conversion rate. This validates the fact that a shortened, concise, and strong value proposition, whether it's mission-focused or offer-focused, is most effective for Hoover's audience. More testing is necessary to determine whether the mission-or-offer-focused value prop works better.

experiments.title: How adding an additional cultivation opportunity affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a checkbox to receive additional content at the end of a form affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2018 - 05/11/2018
Hoover Institution was running their Make America Exceptional Again offer, and wanted to know the effects of offering a checkbox to sign up for the HDR newsletter by the bottom of the form. All copy remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not experience a statistically valid lift. However, this experiment not statistically validating is just as important as if we had seen a lift. This means we can increase the subscribers to the newsletter without adversely impacting acquisition.
The goal of these email acquisition campaigns is to create a file of prospective donors that can also be converted downstream. The best way to do that is through ongoing cultivation.
By getting a new subscriber to opt-in to the ongoing newsletter cultivations, we are putting them into a communication flow that will increase the likelihood of downstream conversion. Since they are having to physically opt-in to the series, it will also increase their perceived value of the communications they will receive.

experiments.title: Does Adding the Word “Free” to a Landing Page Subhead Increase Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding “free” language to the offer page subhead increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2018 - 05/17/2018
Our landing pages for The Hoover Institution’s ebook offers typically focused on simply explaining the benefits of the offer content rather than emphasizing that they are getting it the content for free. Since the typical Hoover donor is usually an older, intellectual, middle-to-upper class demographic, our assumption was that they would likely view a “free” offer as having less value than otherwise. However, we decided to test this hypothesis just in case our assumptions were incorrect.
experiments.keyLearnings: As there was virtually no statistical difference, we concluded that adding "free" language to the subhead was unnecessary to the offer's success. It was not necessarily hurting the value of the offer, but it was also not compelling enough to increase our conversion rate. In this case, we would rather stick with simply relying on the value proposition of the offer to sell itself.

experiments.title: How an additional content offer checkbox affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an additional content offer through a checkbox decrease conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2018 - 05/11/2018
Hoover Institution was offering an eBook, Make America Exceptional Again to build their email file. They also had the Hoover Daily Report, a daily newsletter as an additional content offer. They wanted to give their visitors the chance to opt in to receive the daily newsletter in addition to the eBook, but didn’t want to disrupt conversion on the initial offer.
They decided to set up the checkbox opt-in on a separate page and split the traffic to test the impact of the second offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced no significant impact—increase or decrease—in conversion. Further data analysis showed that 73% of people who saw the treatment also opted into the Hoover Daily Report, which provided additional cultivation and engagement. 

Ultimately, though there wasn't a lift, this test was a win—conversion rate was not affected, and most users chose to receive additional content from Hoover Institution.
